name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayah

```arabic
۞ يَسْـَٔلُونَكَ عَنِ ٱلْأَهِلَّةِ ۖ قُلْ هِىَ مَوَٰقِيتُ لِلنَّاسِ وَٱلْحَجِّ ۗ وَلَيْسَ ٱلْبِرُّ بِأَن تَأْتُوا۟ ٱلْبُيُوتَ مِن ظُهُورِهَا وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱلْبِرَّ مَنِ ٱتَّقَىٰ ۗ وَأْتُوا۟ ٱلْبُيُوتَ مِنْ أَبْوَٰبِهَا ۚ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ (١٨٩)
```

```english
They question you concerning the new moons. Say, ‘They are timekeeping signs for the people and [for the sake of] hajj.’ It is not piety that you enter houses from their rear; rather, piety is [personified by] one who is Godwary, and enter houses from their doors and be wary of Allah, so that you may be felicitous. (189)
```

## (Commentary)

**His saying, the Exalted:** يسئلونك (They ask you) until His saying: والحج (and the Hajj). The term "crescents" (الاهلة) is the plural of "crescent" (هلال). The moon is called a crescent at the beginning of the lunar month when it emerges from beneath the sun's rays on the first and second nights, as has been said. Some have said it applies to the first three nights, while others say it is so called until it "becomes stony" (يتحجر). "Becoming stony" (التحجر) refers to when it forms a complete circle with a fine line. Others have said it is called a crescent until its light overpowers the darkness of the night, which occurs on the seventh night; thereafter it is called a "moon" (قمراً), and on the fourteenth night, it is called a "full moon" (بدراً). Its general name among the Arabs is *al-Zibriqan* (الزبرقان).

The word "crescent" (الهلال) is derived from the phrase "the infant raised a cry" (استهل الصبي) when it cries at birth or shouts. It is also related to their saying: "the people raised their voices for Hajj" (أهل القوم بالحج) when they raise their voices with the *talbiyah* (التلبية) — *the ritual chant of 'Labbayk' performed by pilgrims*. It was named thus because people raise their voices mentioning it when they sight it. "Appointed times" (المواقيت) is the plural of "appointed time" (ميقات), which is the time set for an action. It is also applied to a specific place for an action, such as the *miqat* of the people of Syria or the *miqat* of the people of Yemen, but the first meaning is intended here.

Regarding His saying, the Exalted: **يَسْأَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْأَهِلَّةِ** (They ask you about the new moons), although it does not explicitly state what the question regarding them was—whether it was about the reality of the moon and the cause of its varying phases as a crescent, a moon, and a full moon, as has been suggested; or about the reality of the crescent alone as it appears after the conjunction (المحاق) at the start of the lunar month, as mentioned by some; or about something else.

However, the use of "crescents" (الاهلة) in the question in the plural form, where it says: **يَسْأَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْأَهِلَّةِ** (They ask you about the new moons), is evidence that the question was not about the essence (ماهية) of the moon and its varying phases. Had it been so, it would have been more appropriate to say: "They ask you about the moon" (عن القمر), not "about the crescents." Furthermore, if the question were about the reality of the crescent and the cause of its specific formation, it would have been more appropriate to say: "They ask you about the crescent" (عن الهلال), as there would be no purpose then for the plural. Thus, the use of "crescents" in the plural indicates that the question was about the reason or the benefit in the appearance of the moon as one crescent after another and its marking of the lunar months. It was expressed as "crescents" because they are what realize this function; thus, the answer was given regarding the benefit.

This is further supported by the specific nature of the response: **قُلْ هِيَ مَوَاقِيتُ لِلنَّاسِ وَالْحَجِّ** (Say, ‘They are markers of time for the people and [for the sake of] hajj’). For "appointed times" (المواقيت)—which are the periods set for actions and deeds—are the months themselves, rather than the crescents, which are not periods of time but rather shapes and forms of the moon.

In short, it is concluded that the purpose of the question pertained to the status of the lunar months in terms of their cause or benefit. The response was given by explaining the benefit: that they are times and periods set for people in the affairs of their worldly life and their hereafter. For man, by his very nature (الخلقة), must measure his actions and deeds—all of which are of the nature of motion—by time. This necessitates that the extended time (الزمان الممتد) upon which their affairs are mapped be divided into small and large segments, such as night and day, the day, the month, the seasons, and the years, through Divine care (العناية الالهية) which manages the affairs of His creation and guides them toward the rectitude of their lives. The most apparent division, from which the scholar, the ignorant, the nomad, and the city-dweller alike benefit, and which is easy for everyone to track, is the division of days into lunar months. This is perceived by every person of sound perception and straight senses, unlike the solar months, the significance of which man did not notice, nor did he attain their precise calculation, except after centuries and ages from the beginning of his life on earth; even then, it is not within the capacity of all people at all times.

Thus, the lunar months are set times designated for people in the affairs of their religion and their world, and for the Hajj specifically, as it consists of "known months." It is as if the specific mention of Hajj a second time serves as a prelude to what will be mentioned in the following verses regarding its restriction to certain months.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلَيْسَ الْبِرُّ بِأَنْ تَأْتُوا الْبُيُوتَ مِنْ ظُهُورِهَا** (It is not righteousness that you should come into houses from their backs) until His saying: **مِنْ أَبْوَابِهَا** (from their doors). It is established through transmission (النقل) that a group of the pre-Islamic Arabs, when they entered the state of *ihram* (الإحرام) — *the sacred state of a pilgrim* — for Hajj, would not enter their houses through the door when in need; instead, they would make a hole in the back of the house and enter through it. Islam forbade this and commanded them to enter houses through their doors. The revelation of the verse is consistent with this circumstance; thus, it is correct to rely on what has been transmitted regarding the occasion of revelation (شأن النزول) for this verse, as will be cited later.

Were it not for that, it would be possible to say that His saying: **وَلَيْسَ الْبِرُّ** (It is not righteousness...) to the end, is a metaphor (كناية) for the prohibition against complying with Divine commands and acting upon the legislated laws of the religion except in the manner in which they were legislated. Thus, Hajj is not permissible outside its months, nor is fasting outside the month of Ramadan, and so on. According to this view, the sentence would be a completion of the beginning of the verse, and the meaning would be: these months are set times for deeds legislated within them, and it is not permissible to transgress them to others, such as performing Hajj outside its months or fasting outside the month of Ramadan. Thus, the verse would contain the explanation of a single ruling.

According to the first interpretation, which is supported by transmission, the denial of "righteousness" (البر) for entering houses from their backs indicates that the mentioned act was not something endorsed by the religion; otherwise, there would be no meaning in denying its being "righteousness." Rather, it was a bad pre-Islamic custom, so Allah the Exalted denied its being part of righteousness and affirmed that righteousness is God-consciousness (التقوى) — *the state of godwariness and protection against sin*. It would have been expected to say: "but righteousness is God-consciousness," but the phrasing was changed to: **وَلَكِنَّ الْبِرَّ مَنِ اتَّقَى** (but righteousness is [the quality of] one who is God-conscious), to signal that perfection lies in being characterized by God-consciousness, which is the intended goal, rather than an empty concept, as seen in the parallel verse: **لَيْسَ الْبِرَّ أَنْ تُوَلُّوا وُجُوهَكُمْ قِبَلَ الْمَشْرِقِ وَالْمَغْرِبِ وَلَكِنَّ الْبِرَّ مَنْ آمَنَ** (Piety is not to turn your faces to the east or the west; rather, piety is [personified by] those who have faith...).

The command in His saying, the Exalted: **وَأْتُوا الْبُيُوتَ مِنْ أَبْوَابِهَا** (and come into houses from their doors) is not a masterly command (أمر مولوي) — *a binding legal obligation* — but rather an advisory guidance (إرشاد) toward the propriety of entering houses from their doors, as it aligns with the familiar and commendable custom that accords with the rational purpose (الغرض العقلائي) in building houses and placing doors as points of entry and exit. Since the speech occurs in the context of deterring a bad custom that has no basis other than violating the prevailing custom that accords with rational purpose, it does not indicate more than guidance toward the correct path without making it an obligation (إيجاب). However, entering from other than the door with the intention that it is part of the religion is a forbidden innovation (بدعة).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ** (and be God-conscious, so that you may be felicitous). You have learned at the beginning of the Surah that God-consciousness (التقوى) is among the attributes that encompass all levels of faith and stations of perfection. It is well known that not all stations necessitate felicity (الفلاح) and happiness (السعادة) in the same way as the final stations, which negate polytheism and deviation from their possessor; rather, they lead toward felicity and give tidings of happiness. Therefore, the Exalted said: **وَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ** (and be God-conscious, so that you may be felicitous), using the word of hope (الترجي). It is also possible that what is meant by God-consciousness here is the compliance with this specific command found in the verse and the abandonment of what He censured regarding entering houses from their backs.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Jarir and Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Ibn Abbas, who said: "The people asked the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم about the new moons, so this verse was revealed: **يَسْأَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْأَهِلَّةِ قُلْ هي مَوَاقِيتُ لِلنَّاسِ** (They ask you about the new moons. Say, ‘They are markers of time for the people’), by which they know the term of their debts, the waiting period of their women, and the time of their Hajj."

**I say:** This meaning has been narrated therein through other chains from Abu al-Aliyah, Qatadah, and others. It is also narrated that some of them asked the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم about the different states of the moon, and so the verse was revealed. This is what we mentioned earlier as being contrary to the apparent meaning of the verse, so it is not to be relied upon.

Also in *al-Durr al-Manthur*: Waki', al-Bukhari, and Ibn Jarir narrated from al-Bara: "When they entered the state of *ihram* in the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية), they would enter the house from its back. So Allah revealed: **وَلَيْسَ الْبِرُّ بِأَنْ تَأْتُوا الْبُيُوتَ مِنْ ظُهُورِهَا وَلَكِنَّ الْبِرَّ مَنِ اتَّقَى وَأْتُوا الْبُيُوتَ مِنْ أَبْوَابِهَا** (It is not righteousness that you should come into houses from their backs, but righteousness is [the quality of] one who is God-conscious. And come into houses from their doors)."

Also in *al-Durr al-Manthur*: Ibn Abi Hatim and al-Hakim (who authenticated it) narrated from Jabir, who said: "The Quraysh used to be called *al-Hums* (الحمس), and they would enter through the doors while in *ihram*. The Ansar and the rest of the Arabs would not enter through a door while in *ihram*. While the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم was in a garden, he exited through its door, and Qutbah ibn Amir al-Ansari exited with him. They said: 'O Messenger of Allah, Qutbah ibn Amir is a sinful man, for he exited with you through the door.' He said to him: 'What prompted you to do what you did?' He replied: 'I saw you do it, so I did as you did.' He said: 'I am a *Humsi* man.' He replied: 'Then my religion is your religion.' So Allah revealed: **لَيْسَ الْبِرُّ بِأَنْ تَأْتُوا الْبُيُوتَ مِنْ ظُهُورِهَا** (It is not righteousness that you should come into houses from their backs)."

**I say:** This meaning has been narrated through other chains as well. *Al-Hums* (الحمس) is the plural of *ahmas* (أحمس), like *humr* and *ahmar*, derived from *al-hamasah* (الحماسة), which means intensity or hardness. The Quraysh were named thus due to their intensity in the matter of their religion, or because of their toughness and great might.

The apparent sense of the narration is that the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم had, prior to this incident, endorsed entering from the backs of houses for those other than the Quraysh; hence his questioning him: "What prompted you to do what you did?" etc. Based on this, the verse would be among the abrogating (الناسخ) verses, and it would be abrogating a legislated ruling that was not established by a verse. However, you have already seen that the verse contradicts this, as it says: **لَيْسَ الْبِرُّ بِأَنْ تَأْتُوا** (It is not righteousness that you should come...), and far be it from Allah, the Glorified, that He or His Messenger by His command should legislate a ruling and then censure it or deem it ugly and abrogate it thereafter; this is obvious.

In *al-Mahasin* of al-Barqi, narrated from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him) regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَأْتُوا الْبُيُوتَ مِنْ أَبْوَابِهَا** (and come into houses from their doors), he said: "It means to approach a matter from its proper direction, whatever matter it may be."

In *al-Kafi*, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "The Successors (الاوصياء) are the doors of Allah through which He is approached. Were it not for them, Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, would not be known, and through them, Allah, the Blessed and Exalted, has established His proof over His creation."

**I say:** The narration is of the nature of "application" (الجرى) — *the application of a verse to its most prominent instances* — and an explanation of one of the instances of the verse according to the meaning explained in the first narration. There is no doubt that the verse, in terms of its meaning, is general, even if it is specific in terms of its occasion of revelation. His saying (peace be upon him), "Were it not for them, Allah would not be known," refers to the true explanation and the complete call (الدعوة التامة) that is with them. It has another, more subtle meaning that we may perhaps point to in what is to come, if Allah wills. Narrations carrying the meaning of these two are numerous.
